section.title: The Covenant of Circumcision
section.sectionRationale: Thirteen years after Ishmael's birth, God appears again to confirm and expand the covenant. He gives Abram and Sarai new names, promises a son through Sarah, and seals the covenant in the flesh with the sign of circumcision. This chapter defines Israel's identity as a covenant people.
section.thesis: God binds Himself to Abraham as El-Shaddai, the Almighty, renaming him father of many nations and sealing an everlasting covenant with a sign in the flesh, so that the promise rests on God's power, not human strength.
section.frameNote: Four movements: God Almighty renames Abraham and promises nations; the covenant sign of circumcision; the promise of a son through Sarah and laughter; and same-day obedience.

units.label: God Almighty and a new name

verses.text: When Abram was ninety-nine years old, the LORD appeared to him and said, “I am {{shaddai|God Almighty}}. Walk before Me and be {{blameless|blameless}}.
I will establish My covenant between Me and you, and I will multiply you exceedingly.”
Then Abram fell facedown, and God said to him,
“As for Me, this is My covenant with you: You will be the father of many nations.
No longer will you be called Abram, but your name will be {{abraham|Abraham}}, for I have made you a father of many nations.
I will make you exceedingly fruitful; I will make nations of you, and kings will descend from you.
I will establish My covenant as an {{olam|everlasting}} covenant between Me and you and your descendants after you, to be your God and the God of your descendants after you.
And to you and your descendants I will give the land where you are residing, all the land of Canaan, as an eternal possession; and I will be their God.”
God also said to Abraham, “You must keep My covenant, you and your descendants in the generations after you.
This is My covenant with you and your descendants after you, which you are to keep: Every male among you must be {{circumcise|circumcised}}.
You are to circumcise the flesh of your foreskin, and this will be a sign of the covenant between Me and you.

Generation after generation, every male must be circumcised when he is eight days old, including those born in your household and those purchased from a foreigner, even those who are not your offspring.
Whether they are born in your household or purchased, they must be circumcised. My covenant in your flesh will be an everlasting covenant.
But if any male is not circumcised, he will be cut off from his people; he has broken My covenant.”

units.label: Sarah and the son of laughter

verses.text: Then God said to Abraham, “As for Sarai your wife, do not call her Sarai, for her name is to be Sarah.
And I will bless her and will surely give you a son by her. I will bless her, and she will be the mother of nations; kings of peoples will descend from her.”
Abraham fell facedown. Then he laughed and said to himself, “Can a child be born to a man who is a hundred years old? Can Sarah give birth at the age of ninety?”
And Abraham said to God, “O that Ishmael might live under Your blessing!”
But God replied, “Your wife Sarah will indeed bear you a son, and you are to name him Isaac. I will establish My covenant with him as an everlasting covenant for his descendants after him.
As for Ishmael, I have heard you, and I will surely bless him; I will make him fruitful and multiply him greatly. He will become the father of twelve rulers, and I will make him into a great nation.
But I will establish My covenant with Isaac, whom Sarah will bear to you at this time next year.”
When He had finished speaking with Abraham, God went up from him.

On that very day Abraham took his son Ishmael and all those born in his household or purchased with his money, every male among the members of Abraham’s household, and he circumcised them, just as God had told him.
So Abraham was ninety-nine years old when he was circumcised,
and his son Ishmael was thirteen;
Abraham and his son Ishmael were circumcised on the same day.
And all the men of Abraham’s household, both servants born in his household and those purchased from foreigners, were circumcised with him.

shaddai.gloss: God Almighty, the God of overwhelming power and sufficiency. The name is fitting where God promises life from an aged, barren couple, for the covenant will rest on His might, not their strength. It becomes a favorite name for God among the patriarchs.

blameless.gloss: Whole, complete, of integrity, without blemish. The word is used of sacrificial animals fit for offering and of a life wholly devoted to God. It calls not for sinless perfection but for undivided, single-hearted loyalty to the covenant Lord.

abraham.gloss: The new name means father of a multitude, replacing Abram, exalted father. The change is spoken while he still has only one son by Hagar, so the name is a standing act of faith in a promise not yet visible.

olam.gloss: Everlasting, perpetual, age-enduring. The covenant is called an everlasting covenant, and God binds Himself to be the God of Abraham's line forever. Its ultimate and unbreakable form is the new covenant sealed in Christ's blood.

circumcise.gloss: To cut around, to circumcise. The sign is cut into the very organ of generation, marking that the promised offspring comes by God's power. The prophets and apostles call for a deeper circumcision of the heart, fulfilled in Christ.

basic.context: Abraham is now ninety-nine, thirteen years after Ishmael's birth, and still without the son of promise. God appears again as God Almighty, renames Abram and Sarai, and gives circumcision as the covenant sign. The key question is how sinful people can belong to God, and what mark shows they are His.
basic.hermeneutics: This is covenant law given in story form, and it defines Israel's identity for centuries. The main point is that the covenant rests on God's power and grace, sealed by a sign in the flesh. Christians debate how circumcision relates to baptism and the new covenant, but all agree the outward sign was meant to be matched by an inward reality.
basic.originalLanguages: God calls Himself El-Shaddai, God Almighty, and calls Abraham to be tamim, whole-hearted and blameless. Abram becomes Abraham, father of a multitude, and the covenant is olam, everlasting. The sign is circumcision, cut into the flesh to say the promised offspring comes by God's power alone.
basic.typology: Circumcision, a cutting away of flesh, points to the deeper circumcision of the heart that the prophets promised and that Christ provides. The son given to the as-good-as-dead points to the God who brings life from the dead. Isaac, the child of promise, prefigures the true Son.
basic.christ: Paul says believers are circumcised with a circumcision made without hands, the putting off of the flesh in Christ, and that baptism now marks the people of God, Colossians 2:11-12. Christ is the offspring in whom the everlasting covenant is finally kept.
basic.god: God is the Almighty who makes covenant with weak and aging people, binding Himself to be their God forever. He is faithful to His word, patient with Abraham's laughter, and gracious even toward Ishmael.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 4 shows Abraham received circumcision as a seal of the righteousness he already had by faith. Colossians 2 speaks of circumcision made without hands. Deuteronomy 30 calls for circumcised hearts.
basic.oneStory: Here the covenant is confirmed and marked in the flesh, and the promise narrows to Isaac, the son of laughter. The everlasting covenant runs on to Christ, in whom the true circumcision is of the heart, and in whom people from many nations become children of Abraham.

context.literary: The chapter is structured by God's repeated I will and by the new names given to Abram, Sarai, and the coming son. It matches chapter 15: there God cut the covenant and Abram slept; here God commands the covenant sign and Abraham obeys the same day. Abraham's fall facedown frames both the promise and his laughter.
context.historical: Circumcision was practiced by several ancient peoples, but Israel's was distinctive in its meaning, timing on the eighth day, and covenantal significance. For readers under Moses, this chapter explained the origin and obligation of a rite that set them apart from the nations.
context.canonical: Genesis 17 establishes the covenant sign that runs through the Old Testament and becomes a central issue in the New, where Paul argues that circumcision sealed a righteousness already received by faith and that in Christ a circumcision of the heart replaces the mark in the flesh.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confirm and expand the Abrahamic covenant as everlasting, to establish circumcision as its enduring sign, and to narrow the line of promise to Isaac, born by God's power to an aged couple.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The covenant obligations, including the sign, were prescriptive for Abraham's household and Israel. The New Testament teaches that the physical sign has given way to the reality it pointed to, an inward circumcision of the heart in Christ.

debates.question: How does circumcision relate to the church and to baptism under the new covenant?

views.label: Continuity, circumcision fulfilled and now signified by baptism
views.case: Paul links the two in Colossians 2:11-12; on this view the covenant sign continues in a new form applied to the covenant community, including children.

views.label: Discontinuity, the sign belonged to the old covenant era
views.case: Circumcision marked national Israel and the flesh; in Christ the emphasis falls on personal faith and a new heart, and the physical sign is set aside, Galatians 5:6.

debates.bearing: Both agree the outward mark always aimed at an inward reality, a heart wholly given to God, which Christ alone secures; the difference shapes views of baptism, not the gospel itself.

study.originalLanguages: God reveals Himself as El-Shaddai, God Almighty, the fitting name where life must come from a barren couple. He calls Abraham to be tamim, whole and single-hearted before Him. Abram, exalted father, becomes Abraham, father of a multitude, and Sarai becomes Sarah, princess. The covenant is olam, everlasting, and its sign is mul, circumcision, cut into the flesh so that the promised seed is seen to come by God's power, not man's.

typology.type: Circumcision, the cutting away of flesh
typology.fulfillment: The circumcision of the heart promised by the prophets and given in Christ, the putting off of the body of the flesh, Colossians 2:11.

typology.type: A son promised to the as-good-as-dead
typology.fulfillment: The God who gives life to the dead and calls things that are not as though they were, Romans 4:17, ultimately raising Christ.
Christ, the true offspring of Abraham through whom the everlasting covenant is kept and the nations blessed, Galatians 3:16.

study.christ: The everlasting covenant confirmed here finds its yes in Christ, the promised offspring of Abraham in whom all nations are blessed, Galatians 3:16. Paul explains that Abraham received circumcision as a seal of a righteousness already his by faith, so that he is father of all who believe, Romans 4:11. And believers, Paul says, have been circumcised with a circumcision made without hands, the work of Christ, whose death and resurrection are signified in baptism, Colossians 2:11-12. What was cut in the flesh always pointed to a heart made new.
study.god: God discloses Himself as El-Shaddai, almighty and all-sufficient, willing to covenant with the weak and the old and to stake His own name on their future. He is the faithful God who keeps an everlasting covenant, patient with Abraham's incredulous laughter, generous even to Ishmael, and sovereign in narrowing the promise to Isaac by His own choice rather than human merit.

crossReferences.note: Abraham received circumcision as a seal of the righteousness he had by faith while still uncircumcised.
Believers are circumcised with a circumcision made without hands, joined to Christ's death and resurrection.
The LORD will circumcise your heart so that you will love Him with all your heart.
The promises were spoken to Abraham and to his seed, who is Christ.

study.oneStory: God confirms the covenant as everlasting and marks it in the flesh, narrowing the promise to Isaac, the son of laughter, born by God's power to an aged couple. The sign always pointed beyond itself to a heart made new. The everlasting covenant runs on to Christ, the true offspring, in whom the circumcision of the heart is given and people from every nation become children of Abraham.

quiz.q: What name does God reveal for Himself as He confirms the covenant?

options.text: God Almighty, El-Shaddai, the God of overwhelming power and sufficiency.
options.note: The name fits a promise that must rest on God's power, not human strength.

options.text: The LORD who sees, El-Roi.
options.note: That name belongs to Hagar's story in the previous chapter, not here.

options.text: The Eternal God, El-Olam.
options.note: That title appears later at Beersheba; here the name is El-Shaddai.

options.text: A God who cannot help the barren.
options.note: The whole point is that the Almighty brings life from a barren couple.

quiz.q: What is the purpose of circumcision in this covenant?

options.text: It is a sign in the flesh of the covenant between God and Abraham's line.
options.note: The rite marks belonging to the covenant people.

options.text: It is the means by which Abraham earns God's promise.
options.note: It is a sign of a covenant already given by grace, not a way to earn it.

options.text: It is a health regulation with no deeper meaning.
options.note: The text gives it explicit covenant significance, not merely hygiene.

options.text: It replaces faith as the basis of righteousness.
options.note: Paul says it sealed a righteousness Abraham already had by faith.

quiz.q: Why did God change Abram's name to Abraham?

options.text: Because Abraham means father of a multitude, staking the promise on God's word.
options.note: The new name declares a future not yet visible.

options.text: Because Abraham had already fathered many nations.
options.note: He still had only Ishmael; the name is a promise, not a description of the present.

options.text: Because it was the custom to rename men at ninety-nine.
options.note: There was no such custom; the renaming is God's covenant act.

options.text: Because Abram had failed and needed a fresh start.
options.note: The renaming expresses promise and calling, not disgrace.

quiz.q: How did Abraham respond to the promise of a son through Sarah?

options.text: He fell facedown and laughed, wondering how it could happen at his age.
options.note: His laughter mixes worship and astonishment at the impossible.

options.text: He refused to believe and turned away from God.
options.note: He does struggle, yet he worships and obeys; he does not turn away.

options.text: He demanded a sign before he would believe.
options.note: He asks about Ishmael but does not demand proof here.

options.text: He immediately told Sarah the news with perfect confidence.
options.note: The text records his private laughter, not confident announcement.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament understand circumcision?

options.text: As a seal of righteousness by faith, fulfilled in the circumcision of the heart in Christ.
options.note: Romans 4 and Colossians 2 point to the inward reality in Christ.

options.text: As still required for salvation in the church age.
options.note: Paul explicitly denies this; the sign pointed to Christ, who fulfills it.

options.text: As a rite invented by Israel with no divine meaning.
options.note: God Himself gave it as a covenant sign in this chapter.

options.text: As proof that righteousness comes by works of the flesh.
options.note: It sealed a righteousness already received by faith, apart from works.

questions: God introduced Himself as God Almighty to a couple long past hope of children. Where do you need to trust that God is powerful enough for what looks impossible?
God called Abraham to walk before Him and be blameless, that is, whole-hearted. Is there an area where your loyalty to God is currently divided?
God gave Abraham a new name that spoke of a future he could not yet see. How does it help you to know that God defines you by His promises, not your present circumstances?
The sign in the flesh always pointed to a heart made new. What does it look like for your outward religion to match an inward reality?
Abraham obeyed the very same day, without delay. Is there a step of obedience you have been putting off that God is calling you to take now?

translationNotes.note: God Almighty renders the Hebrew El-Shaddai; the exact derivation is debated, but the sense of overwhelming power and sufficiency is clear from its use.
Abram means exalted father and Abraham means father of many; the wordplay carries the promise, though the exact linguistic form of the new name is discussed by scholars.
Both Sarai and Sarah mean princess; the change in spelling may reflect a difference in dialect and marks her share in the covenant promise.
